raw_response: {"rating": 2.7, "lean": "Lean Right", "explanation": "This is an opinion/editorial that consistently advances a right-leaning skepticism of climate alarm and environmental regulation. The author portrays media, schools, and activists as alarmist and ineffective, emphasizes fossil-fuel-driven prosperity, and argues mitigation policies would have negligible impact — all aligning with conservative narratives. The consistent direction supports a Lean Right rating at +2.7. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author uses dismissive labels for environmental advocates and coverage (e.g., \"joy-sapping environmental alarmism,\" \"climate catastrophists\") and asserts that attention-grabbing climate headlines are not based on sound science, signaling a skeptical, anti-alarm stance; (2) She morally condemns institutions (parents, teachers, corporate media) for imposing misery on children, framing mainstream climate messaging as irresponsible indoctrination; (3) The piece credits modern prosperity \"in large part\" to affordable, reliable fossil fuels and centers that benefit while downplaying costs, reflecting a pro–fossil fuel framing common on the right; (4) It argues anti–fossil fuel policies are futile, claiming even extreme U.S. cuts would make little difference to global temperatures, a core right-leaning case against regulation; (5) The author prefers adaptation over mitigation, urging society to become more adaptable as temperatures rise, a policy preference aligned with conservative approaches to climate; (6) Selective statistics highlight broad human progress and declines in climate-related mortality to rebut catastrophe narratives, with no acknowledgment of countervailing evidence or mainstream scientific consensus — an omission that tilts the framing; (7) Source selection elevates contrarian perspectives and critiques UN/activist climate models as fundamentally flawed, while excluding balancing input from major scientific bodies, reinforcing a one-sided, skeptical view; (8) She asserts the climate will likely remain \"mild and manageable\" and that resiliency will keep improving, explicitly minimizing projected risks without engaging opposing analyses."}

parsed_output.explanation: This is an opinion/editorial that consistently advances a right-leaning skepticism of climate alarm and environmental regulation. The author portrays media, schools, and activists as alarmist and ineffective, emphasizes fossil-fuel-driven prosperity, and argues mitigation policies would have negligible impact — all aligning with conservative narratives. The consistent direction supports a Lean Right rating at +2.7. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author uses dismissive labels for environmental advocates and coverage (e.g., "joy-sapping environmental alarmism," "climate catastrophists") and asserts that attention-grabbing climate headlines are not based on sound science, signaling a skeptical, anti-alarm stance; (2) She morally condemns institutions (parents, teachers, corporate media) for imposing misery on children, framing mainstream climate messaging as irresponsible indoctrination; (3) The piece credits modern prosperity "in large part" to affordable, reliable fossil fuels and centers that benefit while downplaying costs, reflecting a pro–fossil fuel framing common on the right; (4) It argues anti–fossil fuel policies are futile, claiming even extreme U.S. cuts would make little difference to global temperatures, a core right-leaning case against regulation; (5) The author prefers adaptation over mitigation, urging society to become more adaptable as temperatures rise, a policy preference aligned with conservative approaches to climate; (6) Selective statistics highlight broad human progress and declines in climate-related mortality to rebut catastrophe narratives, with no acknowledgment of countervailing evidence or mainstream scientific consensus — an omission that tilts the framing; (7) Source selection elevates contrarian perspectives and critiques UN/activist climate models as fundamentally flawed, while excluding balancing input from major scientific bodies, reinforcing a one-sided, skeptical view; (8) She asserts the climate will likely remain "mild and manageable" and that resiliency will keep improving, explicitly minimizing projected risks without engaging opposing analyses.

explanation: This is an opinion/editorial that consistently advances a right-leaning skepticism of climate alarm and environmental regulation. The author portrays media, schools, and activists as alarmist and ineffective, emphasizes fossil-fuel-driven prosperity, and argues mitigation policies would have negligible impact — all aligning with conservative narratives. The consistent direction supports a Lean Right rating at +2.7. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author uses dismissive labels for environmental advocates and coverage (e.g., "joy-sapping environmental alarmism," "climate catastrophists") and asserts that attention-grabbing climate headlines are not based on sound science, signaling a skeptical, anti-alarm stance; (2) She morally condemns institutions (parents, teachers, corporate media) for imposing misery on children, framing mainstream climate messaging as irresponsible indoctrination; (3) The piece credits modern prosperity "in large part" to affordable, reliable fossil fuels and centers that benefit while downplaying costs, reflecting a pro–fossil fuel framing common on the right; (4) It argues anti–fossil fuel policies are futile, claiming even extreme U.S. cuts would make little difference to global temperatures, a core right-leaning case against regulation; (5) The author prefers adaptation over mitigation, urging society to become more adaptable as temperatures rise, a policy preference aligned with conservative approaches to climate; (6) Selective statistics highlight broad human progress and declines in climate-related mortality to rebut catastrophe narratives, with no acknowledgment of countervailing evidence or mainstream scientific consensus — an omission that tilts the framing; (7) Source selection elevates contrarian perspectives and critiques UN/activist climate models as fundamentally flawed, while excluding balancing input from major scientific bodies, reinforcing a one-sided, skeptical view; (8) She asserts the climate will likely remain "mild and manageable" and that resiliency will keep improving, explicitly minimizing projected risks without engaging opposing analyses.
